text: North Cypress Bariatrics presents a robotic inguinal hernia repair performed by Dr. Brad Snyder.
The patient is positioned supine with arms extended. The abdomen is prepped and draped. A 5mm trocar is inserted into the left upper quadrant for visualization.
The surgeon visualizes all three layers of the abdominal wall and enters the peritoneum, insufflating CO2 gas into the abdomen to a pressure of fifteen millimeters of mercury.
The surgeon initially uses a zero-degree scope to confirm adequate pneumoperitoneum, then switches to a thirty-degree, five-millimeter scope for continued visualization during the procedure.
The surgeon begins the procedure using laparoscopic cameras, transitioning to the robotic camera for the robotic portion of the inguinal hernia repair.
The surgeon identifies the hernia and positions the patient in Trendelenburg, tilting the surgical field to enhance visibility and access to the hernia site.
The surgeon makes an incision to the right side of the umbilicus, attempting to conceal it within the natural creases or folds of the umbilicus, in preparation for repairing a left inguinal hernia.
The surgeon inserts a twelve millimeter trocar into the abdominal cavity for the robotic camera, following the establishment of pneumoperitoneum.
An eight millimeter robotic trocar is placed in the right upper quadrant, replacing the OptiView trocar with another eight millimeter robotic trocar.

The surgeon places a medium-sized Bard 3D Max inguinal hernia mesh designed for the left side into the surgical field. Two ovicral sutures on SH needles are inserted into the abdomen to secure the mesh, with an additional suture prepared in case of breakage.
An external view shows the placement of trocars on the patient's abdomen before the robotic system is docked over the left hip for the hernia repair procedure.
The surgeon positions a fenestrated bipolar instrument in the left hand and a monopolar shear in the right hand, preparing to manipulate tissues within the surgical field.
The surgeon begins the operation by incising and reflecting the peritoneum anterior to the inguinal hernia, exposing the underlying structures for repair.

The surgeon begins working approximately two to three centimeters anterior to the inguinal hernia, extending the dissection laterally onto the transversalis fascia to create a space large enough to accommodate the mesh.
The surgeon identifies the epigastric vessels, which serve as a clear landmark for the procedure.
The surgeon navigates the plane just above the peritoneum, clearly visualizing Cooper's ligament on the pubic tubercle medial to the epigastric vessels.
The surgeon creates space on the anterior surface of the inguinal hernia repair site to place an interrupted vial suture, preparing to tack the mesh securely in position.
The surgeon proceeds to dissect the hernia sac, following the initial takedown of the peritoneum anterior to the inguinal hernia.
Meticulous attention to hemostasis is important here.
The advantages of robotics is superior visualization and articulating monopolar shears. Because of this, we can really control any kind of bleeding before it begins and gets our field bloody.
In addition to that, less blood in the field and less blood when the patient's healing probably relates to less pain post-operatively.
The surgeon uses monopolar shears and blunt dissection to detach tissue attachments near the epigastric vessels, carefully approaching the area while maintaining control over the dissection.
The surgeon encounters the edge of the internal inguinal ring, with the hernia visibly protruding into the ring.
The surgeon carefully manipulates the anterior surface of the hernia sac, ensuring not to injure it, while preparing to reveal the cord structures posteriorly.

The surgeon continues working in the same plane, using blunt dissection to push adhesions and preperitoneal fat away from the hernia sac. The edge of the hernia sac is visible, indicating the hernia is not very large or long.
The surgeon scores the tissue to initiate a dissection plane, revealing the cord structures beneath as they come into view.

The surgeon alternates between working on one side and then the other, gradually reducing the hernia sac while ensuring good hemostasis. The sac is carefully manipulated to minimize bleeding during the reduction process.

The surgeon exposes the base of the hernia sac, revealing cleaner tissue planes. A clear inferior edge is created for the mesh placement into the preperitoneal space. The transversalis fascia is visible laterally.
The surgeon examines the mesh, demonstrating how it retracts back into the hernia site, indicating that the hernia is not yet reduced.
The surgeon intermittently pulls the hernia sac out to visually inspect and identify any remaining attachments holding it in place.

The surgeon carefully works on the posterior edge of the hernia sac, paying close attention to the nearby cord structures, including the gonadal vessels and spermatic cord, which are visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon slows the dissection, meticulously working to release the hernia sac from the cord structures. A window is created, revealing the cord structures below, with the cremasteric fibers visible anteriorly.
The surgeon uses energy sources to control bleeding from the cremasteric fibers while releasing the cord vessels. The vas deferens is visible in an inferior position relative to the cord structures.
The surgeon identifies the vas deferens positioned at the edge of the hernia sac, demonstrating confidence in the dissection process.

The surgeon cauterizes the bleeding cremasteric vessels and carefully pushes the cord structures down, avoiding injury to the surrounding structures in the triangle of doom. The iliac vessels are visible beneath the cord structures.
The surgeon proceeds with caution while dissecting posteriorly, revealing the femoral and iliac vessels beneath the surgical field.
The surgeon completes the reduction of the hernia sac and prepares to place mesh into the dissected area using two 5mm needle droppers, with assistance from the scrub assistant.
The surgeon uses blunt dissection with needle droppers to create lateral space for mesh placement, addressing incomplete dissection in the area.
The surgeon places the mesh towards the pubic tubercle and pushes it into position, ensuring it lays correctly over the dissected area.
The surgeon uses a 2-0 vascular suture on an SH needle to tack the mesh to the pubic tubercle, carefully placing the stitch in the challenging-to-visualize area.

The surgeon uses the shaft of the needle driver to push medial tissue aside for better visualization. A suture is placed with a half-centimeter bite on the apex of the mesh, securing it to the pubic tubercle. The surgeon maintains tissue displacement with the needle driver while tying the knot to ensure clear visibility.
The surgeon uses two needle drivers to break sutures by grasping them firmly and turning the drivers ninety degrees away from each other, demonstrating the technique twice more in the video.

I prefer the five millimeter snaked needle drivers because of their size and their dexterity. However, you do have to be careful sometimes throwing knots that you don't wrap the suture around the snake articulating portions because it will break the suture quite easily if you're not careful.
The surgeon ties a few knots to secure the mesh in place over the pubic tubercle, using three sutures to maintain its orientation until the mesh can scar in naturally.

The surgeon positions the top edge of the mesh underneath the area where the peritoneum will be closed on the anterior surface. A suture is placed at the edge of the mesh, securing it with a loose air knot.

The surgeon gently ties the mesh in place without excessive tension, ensuring it holds securely. The needle is inserted deeply into the abdominal wall to prevent it from being lost during the procedure.
The surgeon uses a V-lock absorbable barbed suture to begin closing the peritoneum from the medial aspect.

The surgeon cinches down the peritoneum using a suture technique, ensuring that the peritoneum is tightly closed without gaps. Every third throw of the Vicryl suture is locked to maintain closure integrity.
The surgeon uses a barbed suture to effectively hold and close the peritoneal tissue, ensuring there are no gaps that could expose the underlying mesh to the bowel.
The surgeon performs a running suture across the peritoneal defect, using a backhand technique from bottom to top to approximate the inferior edge of the peritoneum superiorly or anteriorly.
The surgeon assesses the hernia sac and incorporates it into the closure of the peritoneum using a single suture bite, effectively pulling up the sac to protect the peritoneal closure.
The surgeon uses a barbed suture to secure the peritoneum, pulling the suture laterally to remove slack and then cinching it down medially to ensure a tight closure without tearing the tissue.
The surgeon uses a suture with memory, employing a pulley technique to pull the needle down and push it with one hand, effectively removing slack from the suture while maintaining camera position.

The surgeon reverses the direction of the barbed suture to lock it into place, completing the closure of the peritoneum without knots. The mesh remains intact, and the robotic system is undocked. The needles are removed, and the fascia is closed.
I hope you enjoyed it. Watch more at NorthCypressBariatrics.com.
